description: 62M syncope at the gym, no clear cause, normal exam — SF Syncope candidate
transcript:
Dr. Patel: Tell me what happened.
Patient: I was at the gym, finished a set on the treadmill, walked over to get water, and the next thing I remember I was on the floor with someone standing over me.
Dr. Patel: How long were you out?
Bystander: Maybe twenty seconds. He came right back.
Dr. Patel: Any warning? Lightheaded, sweaty, nausea?
Patient: Not really. I just felt off and then it went black.
Dr. Patel: Any chest pain, palpitations, shortness of breath before or after?
Patient: No.
Dr. Patel: Did you hit your head?
Patient: A little, on my shoulder mostly. Maybe a small bump on the back of my head.
Dr. Patel: Any prior episodes like this?
Patient: Never.
Dr. Patel: Past medical history?
Patient: High blood pressure, on lisinopril. That's it.
Dr. Patel: Family history of sudden death, heart disease in young people?
Patient: My dad had a heart attack at 70, but that's it.
Dr. Patel: Any shortness of breath day to day, leg swelling?
Patient: No.
Nurse: HR 78, BP 132/82, sat 98, temp normal.
Dr. Patel: EKG looks like normal sinus, no ischemia, normal intervals. Lungs clear. Heart sounds regular, no murmur. No focal neuro findings, alert and oriented.
Dr. Patel: Hematocrit will be back in a few minutes. He looks well now. Let me think about whether we can risk-stratify and dispo or whether this needs admission.

groundTruth.notes: Classic SF Syncope use case. True syncope (LOC with spontaneous recovery), no clear benign cause, doctor explicitly weighing dispo decision. CHESS criteria are the right structure. HEART should NOT fire — this is syncope workup, not chest pain workup, and there is no anginal symptom.
